posts.cooked:
2008 Mâcon-Burgy; Domaine de Chervin: Albert Goyard & Fils
earth, perfume, cherry, plum, spice and pepper. Round and ripe, fairly deep and powerful. A touch of bubblegum that bounces about a little from time to time. A nice wine but lacks true complexity, but I am finding Macon Rouge far deeper and more complex than their Beaujolais twins! ***(+) or [4+4+4+4=16/20]

posts.name: Scott Smith
posts.cooked:
Is this Gamay or Pinot or a blend?

Thanks for the label pic - that is always a help when I read an interesting note about something that I’ve never heard of.

posts.name: Michael Mager
posts.cooked:
Was also curious about the varietal – per CT, it’s Gamay…

Michael

Pretty much all Macon Rouge is Gamay I believe.

I think it is a better source than Beaujolais for both complexity and depth and perhaps shows how important terroir is.

posts.name: Laurent Gibet
posts.cooked:
Pretty much all Macon Rouge is Gamay I believe.

I think it is a better source than Beaujolais for both complexity and depth and perhaps shows how important terroir is.

Mâcon Bugy uses gamay.

I discovered the wines of this producer yesterday :
Domaine de Chervin Viré-Clessé (Albert Goyard) En Châtelaine 2015 : 15/20 – 14/11/2020 (good wine)
Curieux, du végétal, beaucoup d’acidité (mordante, presque dérangeante), très chenin initialement (plus coing qu’ananas). Pointe d’oxydation.
Le lendemain, hyper nerveux, un peu chardonnay du Jura, citronné, légèrement grillé, avec une pointe oxydative.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Pretty much all Macon Rouge is Gamay I believe.

I think it is a better source than Beaujolais for both complexity and depth and perhaps shows how important terroir is.

Yes, that’s right regarding the grape variety; but I think the differences in style have as much to do with vine genetics as with “terroir”. The Mâconnais historically produced a lot of red wine. But when Mâcon (and especially Pouilly-Fuissé) exploded internationally post-war, the incentive to replant to Chardonnay was enormous. Just as in Chassagne-Montrachet, if people kept any red grapevines, it tended to be their oldest and best vines. So, in places such as Mâcon-Cruzille you can find really interesting small- and open-clustered massale selections of Gamay that can make great wine. Add the freshness that comes from vines growing in basic soils, and the results are exciting and age-worthy.

posts.name: Richard T r i m p i
posts.cooked:
Cool. Where is the dividing line between Gamay and Pinot Noir? Tornus - Sercy - St. Clement sur Guye?

RT
posts.display_username: Richard T r i m p i

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Pretty much all Macon Rouge is Gamay I believe.

I think it is a better source than Beaujolais for both complexity and depth and perhaps shows how important terroir is.

Yes, that’s right regarding the grape variety; but I think the differences in style have as much to do with vine genetics as with “terroir”. The Mâconnais historically produced a lot of red wine. But when Mâcon (and especially Pouilly-Fuissé) exploded internationally post-war, the incentive to replant to Chardonnay was enormous. Just as in Chassagne-Montrachet, if people kept any red grapevines, it tended to be their oldest and best vines. So, in places such as Mâcon-Cruzille you can find really interesting small- and open-clustered massale selections of Gamay that can make great wine. Add the freshness that comes from vines growing in basic soils, and the results are exciting and age-worthy.

That’s pretty cool. Do you know any specific vignerons there?

Guillot-Broux - serious wines that need a bit of age for their acid-tannin structure to soften, have had them back the the 1980s

Domaine de Vignes du Maynes - their Manganite gets my vote as the best Mâconnias red, but they are in the “natural” camp so your mileage may vary, especially overseas

Domaine de la Sarazinière

Domaine Sainte Barbe

And if you want an insight into Mâcon-Cruzille, where the first two producers are based, you might enjoy this video I made:

https://www.instagram.com/p/CGFf0TRKMhG/

And if you want an insight into Mâcon-Cruzille, where the first two producers are based, you might enjoy this video I made:

William Kelley on Instagram: "For something a little different, this video explores the overgrown clos in the woods above Mâcon-Cruzille: enclosed vineyards, cultivated for centuries, which were abandoned after phylloxera. I don’t know of a more tangible example of how this insect, which devastated vineyards in the late 19th century, changed Burgundy’s viticultural landscape. #phylloxera #burgundy"

I agree for Clos des Vignes du Maynes. Very good whites (that seem to travel well in my experience) and reds at the domain. But I had some disappointment with the reds after travelling :
Julien Guillot Clos des Vignes du Maynes Bourgogne rouge Les Crays 2018 (flawed after a few glasses, I was alone with this bottle).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Very good whites (that seem to travel well in my experience)

I have had issues with protein instabilities in the whites, which is something I really don’t like.

I don’t know how to be more explicit… unless you are asking me to explain what protein instability (or “casse” in French) is?

posts.name: Laurent Gibet
posts.cooked:
I have had issues with protein instabilities in the whites, which is something I really don’t like.

William,

Could you be more explicit ?

Thank you

I don’t know how to be more explicit… unless you are asking me to explain what protein instability (or “casse” in French) is?

Casse protéique … défaut visuel …

La casse protéique, définie comme une modification de la structure tridimensionnelle des protéines sous l’action de la chaleur puis une insolubilité aux températures ‘froides’, est un défaut esthétique visible dans les vins blancs. Cette apparition n’a aucune influence sur la qualité du vin ou sur la santé du consommateur mais un vrai blocage se fait à l’achat si le vin présente ce défaut. Les laboratoires utilisent différentes méthodes pour déterminer la dose de bentonite nécessaire à la stabilité protéique du vin.

Protein cassia, defined as a modification of the three-dimensional structure of proteins under the action of heat and then insolubility at “cold” temperatures, is an aesthetic defect visible in white wines. This appearance has no influence on the quality of the wine or on the health of the consumer, but a real blockage is made when purchasing if the wine has this defect. Laboratories use different methods to determine the dose of bentonite necessary for protein stability in wine.

Never seen this sort of milky veil, on Julien Guillot’s whites …

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Never seen this sort of milky veil, on Julien Guillot’s whites …

Well, I discussed it with Julien, and he told me how he intended to avoid it in the future! But maybe he and I were both mistaken, and he never had any issues

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
This appearance has no influence on the quality of the wine

Also, I entirely disagree with this definition. There’s a solid precipitate in the wine, which totally ruins its texture. I find wines suffering from protein instability undrinkable.

posts.name: Laurent Gibet
posts.cooked:
This appearance has no influence on the quality of the wine

Also, I entirely disagree with this definition. There’s a solid precipitate in the wine, which totally ruins its texture. I find wines suffering from protein instability undrinkable.

OK …
A friend of mine, oenologist, tells me that this only solution is to use bentonite (argile). Chardonnay should be more resistant to this “casse” than other grape varieties.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
This appearance has no influence on the quality of the wine

Also, I entirely disagree with this definition. There’s a solid precipitate in the wine, which totally ruins its texture. I find wines suffering from protein instability undrinkable.

OK …
A friend of mine, oenologist, tells me that this only solution is to use bentonite (argile). Chardonnay should be more resistant to this “casse” than other grape varieties.

Elevage in oak also helps with protein stability, as oak tannins react with proteins.
